title: 1 - 📊 Engineering Overview
description: Welcome page — dashboard index, architecture overview, schema drift status, and data sync controls.

targets.rawSql: SELECT COALESCE('Live: ' || (SELECT value FROM app_config WHERE key = 'org') || '/' || (SELECT value FROM app_config WHERE key = 'repo'), 'Not yet synced') AS "Data Source"

options.content:
## 📋 Dashboard Index

| # | Dashboard | Purpose | Tags |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | [📊 Engineering Overview](/d/overview) | Dashboard index, architecture overview, schema drift status, and data sync controls | Overview |
| 2 | [🤖 Copilot Adoption](/d/edu-copilot-adoption) | Active seats, acceptance rate, usage by editor | Copilot |
| 3 | [👤 Per-User Copilot Metrics](/d/edu-per-user-copilot) | Acceptance rate and activity per developer | Copilot |
| 4 | [🏛️ Organization Copilot Leading Indicators](/d/edu-organization-leading) | PR activity, review engagement, and merge time — org-scoped | Copilot, Leading |
| 5 | [🏢 Enterprise Copilot Leading Indicators](/d/edu-enterprise-leading) | PR activity, review engagement, and merge time — enterprise-scoped | Copilot, Leading |
| 6 | [📉 Enterprise Lagging Indicators](/d/edu-enterprise-lagging) | DORA outcomes: deployment frequency, lead time, CFR, MTTR — enterprise scope | DORA, Lagging |

---

## 🏗️ Why ELT — not ETL

Most production dashboards use **ETL** (Extract → **Transform** → Load): data is reshaped by application code before it ever reaches the database. That keeps queries simple, but it hides what the underlying API actually returns — intermediate column names, flattened structures, and computed fields make it hard to understand what you're really measuring or to adapt the pipeline to a new org.

This tool is built for **learning**, not production hardening. It uses **ELT** (Extract → Load → **Transform**):

| Step | Where it happens | What it means |
|---|---|---|
| **Extract** | `src/github/*.ts` | Fetch raw JSON from the GitHub API via Octokit |
| **Load** | PostgreSQL | Store fields verbatim — column names match the API field names exactly |
| **Transform** | Grafana SQL panels | All aggregations, joins, and calculations live here, in plain SQL |

Because the schema mirrors the API, you can read a dashboard SQL panel and trace every field back to the GitHub documentation. There are no hidden mappings or invented column names.

```
GitHub API  →  Octokit SDK (TypeScript)  →  PostgreSQL  →  Grafana SQL
               src/github/*.ts               db/schema.sql   dashboards/*.json
```

**Auto-migration** — when GitHub adds a new field, the next sync detects it and runs `ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN` automatically. The _Schema Drift_ section below tracks what has been added.

**Data refresh** — click **Refresh Data Now** to pull the latest 28-day rolling window from GitHub.

## ⚙️ Environment

| Variable | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `GITHUB_TOKEN` | Classic PAT — needs `repo`, `read:org`, `admin:org` scopes |
| `GITHUB_ENTERPRISE` | Enterprise slug (e.g. `Octodemo`) |
| `GITHUB_ORG` | Organisation name |
| `GITHUB_REPO` | Repository name |

Why is a sync needed? Data is pulled from the GitHub API in a rolling 28-day window and stored locally in PostgreSQL. It does not update automatically. Click Refresh Data Now to fetch the latest PRs, deployments, workflow runs, issues, and Copilot metrics (~15–30 s). The sync history table below shows recent jobs, their status, and how many records were written.

targets.rawSql: SELECT UPPER(status) AS "Last Sync Status" FROM sync_jobs ORDER BY started_at DESC LIMIT 1
SELECT COALESCE((SELECT SUM(v::int) FROM jsonb_each_text(records_synced) x(k,v)), 0)::bigint AS "Records Synced" FROM sync_jobs ORDER BY started_at DESC LIMIT 1

options.content: Calls POST /sync · sync server on localhost:3005

targets.rawSql: SELECT TO_CHAR(started_at AT TIME ZONE 'UTC', 'YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI') || ' UTC' AS "Last Sync" FROM sync_jobs ORDER BY started_at DESC LIMIT 1

SELECT id AS "Job", status AS "Status", TO_CHAR(started_at AT TIME ZONE 'UTC', 'YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI') AS "Started", COALESCE((SELECT SUM(v::int) FROM jsonb_each_text(records_synced) x(k,v)), 0)::bigint AS "Records" FROM sync_jobs ORDER BY id DESC LIMIT 10

options.content:
Schema drift happens when the GitHub API adds new fields. On each sync the app auto-runs ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN, so the live DB stays current — but two follow-ups remain, tracked by the tables below:
  • not yet in schema.sql — codify the columns in schema.sql so fresh rebuilds include them.
  • not yet visualized — no Grafana panel references them yet.
Click Ignore in either table to hide a column you don't intend to act on; it moves to the Ignored drift columns table where you can Unignore it later. Caveat: ignores are stored in postgres only — running docker compose down -v wipes them. See the Workflow section at the bottom to update both.

panels.title: schema.sql definition
panels.description: Counts drift columns (across all syncs) that exist in the live database but are not yet codified in schema.sql. Zero means a fresh rebuild will recreate every drift column.

targets.rawSql:
WITH drift_cols AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT
    entry->>'table' AS table_name,
    col->>'field'   AS column_name
  FROM sync_jobs j,
       jsonb_array_elements(j.schema_drift) entry,
       jsonb_array_elements(entry->'auto_applied') col
  WHERE j.schema_drift IS NOT NULL
    AND j.schema_drift <> '[]'::jsonb
)
SELECT COALESCE(COUNT(*)::int, 0) AS "Missing from schema.sql"
FROM drift_cols dc
LEFT JOIN schema_columns s
  ON s.table_name = dc.table_name AND s.column_name = dc.column_name
LEFT JOIN drift_ignores i
  ON i.table_name = dc.table_name AND i.column_name = dc.column_name AND i.scope = 'schema'
WHERE s.column_name IS NULL
  AND i.column_name IS NULL

panels.title: Visualization
panels.description: Counts drift columns (across all syncs) that no Grafana panel's SQL references. Zero means every drift column is surfaced somewhere in a dashboard.

targets.rawSql:
WITH drift_cols AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT
    entry->>'table' AS table_name,
    col->>'field'   AS column_name
  FROM sync_jobs j,
       jsonb_array_elements(j.schema_drift) entry,
       jsonb_array_elements(entry->'auto_applied') col
  WHERE j.schema_drift IS NOT NULL
    AND j.schema_drift <> '[]'::jsonb
),
referenced AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT dc.table_name, dc.column_name
  FROM drift_cols dc
  JOIN dashboard_panel_sql d
    ON d.raw_sql ILIKE '%' || dc.column_name || '%'
)
SELECT COALESCE(COUNT(*)::int, 0) AS "Not visualized"
FROM drift_cols dc
LEFT JOIN referenced r
  ON r.table_name = dc.table_name AND r.column_name = dc.column_name
LEFT JOIN drift_ignores i
  ON i.table_name = dc.table_name AND i.column_name = dc.column_name AND i.scope = 'panel'
WHERE r.column_name IS NULL
  AND i.column_name IS NULL

panels.title: Drift columns not yet in schema.sql
panels.description: Columns added at runtime by applyDrift() that have not been codified into schema.sql. Until they are, a fresh database build will not include them — fix by running the schema-drift-apply skill.

targets.rawSql:
WITH drift_cols AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT
    entry->>'table'        AS table_name,
    col->>'field'          AS column_name,
    MAX(j.started_at)      AS last_seen
  FROM sync_jobs j,
       jsonb_array_elements(j.schema_drift) entry,
       jsonb_array_elements(entry->'auto_applied') col
  WHERE j.schema_drift IS NOT NULL
    AND j.schema_drift <> '[]'::jsonb
  GROUP BY 1, 2
)
SELECT
  dc.table_name                                         AS "Table",
  dc.column_name                                        AS "Field Added",
  TO_CHAR(dc.last_seen AT TIME ZONE 'UTC',
          'YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI')                         AS "First Seen",
  'Ignore'                                              AS "Ignore"
FROM drift_cols dc
LEFT JOIN schema_columns s
  ON s.table_name = dc.table_name AND s.column_name = dc.column_name
LEFT JOIN drift_ignores i
  ON i.table_name = dc.table_name AND i.column_name = dc.column_name AND i.scope = 'schema'
WHERE s.column_name IS NULL
  AND i.column_name IS NULL
ORDER BY dc.last_seen DESC, dc.table_name, dc.column_name

defaults.noValue: ✓ All drift columns are codified in schema.sql

value.title: Hide this drift column from the schema.sql table above

panels.title: Drift columns not yet visualized
panels.description: Columns added by applyDrift() that no panel's rawSql references. A drift column is fully resolved only when (a) it is in schema.sql AND (b) at least one dashboard panel uses it.

targets.rawSql:
WITH drift_cols AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT
    entry->>'table'        AS table_name,
    col->>'field'          AS column_name,
    MAX(j.started_at)      AS last_seen
  FROM sync_jobs j,
       jsonb_array_elements(j.schema_drift) entry,
       jsonb_array_elements(entry->'auto_applied') col
  WHERE j.schema_drift IS NOT NULL
    AND j.schema_drift <> '[]'::jsonb
  GROUP BY 1, 2
),
referenced AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT dc.table_name, dc.column_name
  FROM drift_cols dc
  JOIN dashboard_panel_sql d
    ON d.raw_sql ILIKE '%' || dc.column_name || '%'
)
SELECT
  dc.table_name                                         AS "Table",
  dc.column_name                                        AS "Field Added",
  TO_CHAR(dc.last_seen AT TIME ZONE 'UTC',
          'YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI')                         AS "First Seen",
  'Ignore'                                              AS "Ignore"
FROM drift_cols dc
LEFT JOIN referenced r
  ON r.table_name = dc.table_name AND r.column_name = dc.column_name
LEFT JOIN drift_ignores i
  ON i.table_name = dc.table_name AND i.column_name = dc.column_name AND i.scope = 'panel'
WHERE r.column_name IS NULL
  AND i.column_name IS NULL
ORDER BY dc.last_seen DESC, dc.table_name, dc.column_name

defaults.noValue: ✓ All drift columns are referenced by at least one panel

value.title: Hide this drift column from the visualization table above

panels.title: Ignored drift columns
panels.description: Drift columns the user has chosen to hide from the two tables above. Click 'Unignore' to restore. NOTE: this list is stored in postgres only — `docker compose down -v` clears it.

targets.rawSql:
SELECT
  table_name                                         AS "Table",
  column_name                                        AS "Field Added",
  CASE scope
    WHEN 'schema' THEN 'schema.sql'
    WHEN 'panel'  THEN 'visualization'
    ELSE scope
  END                                                AS "Scope",
  scope                                              AS "_scope",
  TO_CHAR(created_at AT TIME ZONE 'UTC',
          'YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI')                       AS "Ignored At",
  'Unignore'                                          AS "Unignore"
FROM drift_ignores
ORDER BY created_at DESC, table_name, column_name, scope

value.title: Restore this drift column to the table above

panels.title: Human-in-the-loop workflow — When new fields are added

options.content:
Auto-migration keeps the live database current, but turning a new API field into a useful metric is a **human-in-the-loop** workflow. **Each agent-driven step below requires human validation before moving on** — the agent infers types from naming conventions and infers metric shape from column profiles, but only you can confirm:

- the inferred PostgreSQL **column types are correct** (e.g., is `*_count` really `BIGINT`, or is it a UUID-shaped `TEXT`?),
- the proposed **visualization is meaningful and accurate** (right aggregation, right time grain, right caveats), and
- the change belongs in this product at all (some new API fields are noise, not signal).

Follow these steps when the *Drift columns not yet in schema.sql* or *Drift columns not yet visualized* tables above show new columns.

### 1. Detect
Watch the two tables above — **Drift columns not yet in schema.sql** and **Drift columns not yet visualized**. Any row in either table is a column the GitHub API recently returned, that `applyDrift()` has added to the live database, and that still needs to be codified or surfaced.

### 2. Codify the schema
Ask the agent: **“run the `schema-drift-apply` skill for table `<table>` fields `<field1>, <field2>`.”**
The skill patches `schema.sql`, **rebuilds the sync-server Docker image**, and verifies the columns. Rows clear from the *Drift columns not yet in schema.sql* table above only after the rebuild — a plain `docker-compose restart` is not enough because `schema.sql` is COPY'd into the image.

👁️ **Validate before approving:** the agent infers PostgreSQL types from the column name (e.g. `*_count` → `BIGINT`, `*_at` → `TIMESTAMPTZ`). Confirm each inferred type matches the actual API payload — a misclassified column will silently truncate or reject data on the next sync.

### 3. Propose a metric
Ask the agent: **“run the `drift-to-metric-plan` skill for table `<table>` fields `<field1>, <field2>`.”**
The skill profiles the column in the live DB, classifies it (counter, adoption %, JSONB breakdown, recency, etc.), picks a target dashboard, and prints a Markdown panel proposal — draft SQL, suggested visualization, Learning Guide content, and any caveats. **It does not edit any dashboard JSON.**

### 4. Review
Read the proposal carefully. **This is the most important human checkpoint.** Confirm the SQL aggregation matches what the field actually represents, the visualization type fits the data shape, and the Learning Guide accurately describes the metric's caveats and limitations. Reject or revise anything that looks like a plausible-but-wrong inference. The skill will offer alternatives when classification is ambiguous.

### 5. Implement
If you approve a panel, ask the agent to apply it to the chosen dashboard JSON. The agent then runs `docker-compose up -d --build sync-server` from the repo root so the new dashboard JSON is baked into the image and re-indexed into `dashboard_panel_sql`. **Once it's running, open the dashboard in Grafana and visually verify the rendered chart matches expectations.** From the repo root, run `npm test && npm run lint && npm run build` — all three must pass.

### 6. Ship
Open a pull request that includes the `schema.sql` change and the dashboard JSON change.

---

❗ **Both skills are local-only.** They never push code, force-merge, or open PRs on their own. Every step above is gated by you.

⚠️ **Caveat:** both the schema definition and `.json` dashboard files are baked into the Docker image. Editing them requires `docker-compose up -d --build sync-server` from the repo root — a plain `restart` reuses the old image and the *Drift columns not yet in schema.sql* and *Drift columns not yet visualized* tables above will look unchanged.

📚 Skill files: `.github/skills/schema-drift-apply/SKILL.md` and `.github/skills/drift-to-metric-plan/SKILL.md`.
